name: Sadyojyotis
dates_notes: Earliest dualist Saiddhāntika theologian whose works survive; dated by internal references and citation in Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa.

lineage_polemical.note: Somānanda's Śiva-Dṛṣṭi inaugurates the Pratyabhijñā non-dualist position against the dualist Saiddhāntika ontology of pati / paśu / pāśa for which Sadyojyotis is the early authoritative exponent.
Utpaladeva's Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā extends Somānanda's polemic against the three-fold dualist ontology Sadyojyotis defends.

core_thesis: Sadyojyotis is the earliest surviving systematic theologian of the Kashmirian *Śaiva-Siddhānta* — a tradition distinct from both the southern Tamil Śaiva-Siddhānta and from the later non-dual Pratyabhijñā, and standing as positive metaphysics in its own right rather than as a preliminary stage to non-dualism. The position is *bhedavāda*: *pati* (Śiva, the supreme Lord), *paśu* (the bound soul), and *pāśa* (the three bonds — *āṇava*, *māyīya*, *kārma*) are three eternally distinct, irreducible, positively existent realities (*Tattva-Trayanirṇaya* vv. 1–10, the foundational disposition; [*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 1.1–25](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/1.1-25) establishing the threefold ontology). Śiva is omniscient, omnipotent, and transcendent of *māyā* ([*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 2.1–40](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/2.1-40)); the soul is intrinsically *cit*, intrinsically endowed with *kartṛtva* and *bhoktṛtva*, but constitutively bound by *āṇavamala* — an actual ontological obscuration, a real *dravya*-substance covering an intrinsically real *cit*-substance, not a *jīva*-side misperception that could be dispelled by gnosis alone (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 1–25; *Mokṣa-Kārikā* vv. 1–20 on the substantial character of *mala*). Liberation (*mokṣa*) consists in the soul's actual purification from *mala* through Śiva-bestowed *dīkṣā* and the consequent attainment of equality (*samya*) of powers with Śiva, while remaining numerically distinct from him (*Mokṣa-Kārikā* vv. 30–60, the *samya* doctrine; [*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 3.1–40](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/3.1-40) on the eternal distinction at *mukti*). *Mukti* is therefore neither absorption nor identity-recognition but an eternal real distinction with full siddha-powers — a structurally pluralist soteriology comparable in its commitment to the eternity-of-the-individual to Madhva's *jīva-traividhya* and to Leibniz's monadic plurality, though articulated within an explicitly theist Śaiva theology rather than within either Vedānta or Western metaphysics. The corpus argues this against Buddhist no-self ([*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 1.10–25](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/1.10-25)), against Sāṃkhya puruṣa-pluralism (*Tattva-Saṅgraha* vv. 1–15, on the ground that an unconscious *prakṛti* cannot account for purposive bondage and release), and against any consciousness-as-sole-substance reading: the world is real (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 30–60 on *māyā* as real material substrate, not as illusion), the soul is real ([*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 1.30–60](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/1.30-60)), and Śiva is real ([*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 2.1–40](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/2.1-40)), in three distinct ontological registers that no monism can collapse without loss.

engaged_works.title: Bhoga-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; one of the four foundational kārikā-treatises of the early dualist Saiddhāntika theology.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Watson, Goodall, Filliozat editions and studies).

engaged_works.summary: Verse-treatise on *bhoga* — experience or enjoyment by the bound soul — as the structural condition the Saiddhāntika system must explain. Sadyojyotis argues that *bhoga* requires three eternally real factors: a *bhoktṛ* (the soul, *paśu*), a *bhogya* (the experienceable world, derived from *māyā* as a real material principle), and a *prerakṛ* (Śiva, who, by his own grace, conjoins soul and world for the soul's spiritual maturation) (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 1–25, the foundational threefold). The argument is that no monist ontology — neither consciousness-only nor *prakṛti*-only — can ground real *bhoga*: only an irreducible three-fold ontology suffices (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 26–55). The text is the analytic foundation for the position that *māyā* in Śaiva-Siddhānta is not illusion but a real material substrate (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 56–85), and that bondage is a real ontological condition rather than a cognitive error (*Bhoga-Kārikā* vv. 86–110). Read together with the *Mokṣa-Kārikā*, it gives the dualist Saiddhāntika account of how *saṃsāra* is structured and why liberation requires Śiva's actual intervention rather than mere gnosis.

engaged_works.title: Moksha-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored companion to the Bhoga-Kārikā.
engaged_works.summary: Verse-treatise on *mokṣa* as the structural counterpart to the *Bhoga-Kārikā*'s account of *bhoga*. Sadyojyotis argues that liberation is the soul's *actual* removal of *āṇavamala* — the constitutive obscuring impurity — together with attainment of equality (*samya*) of powers with Śiva: omniscience, omnipotence-through-grace, and freedom from the bonds (*Mokṣa-Kārikā* vv. 30–60, the *samya* doctrine). *Mokṣa* is therefore neither identity with Śiva (against the Pratyabhijñā non-dualists who have not yet appeared on the scene) nor mere *jñāna* (against the Vedāntic identification of liberation with knowledge): it is an objective ontological transformation of the soul, performed by Śiva through *dīkṣā* and consummated at death (*Mokṣa-Kārikā* vv. 61–110 on the *dīkṣā*-mediated transformation). The liberated soul retains its individual identity eternally and is not absorbed into Śiva (*Mokṣa-Kārikā* vv. 111–end). The text is foundational for the dualist Saiddhāntika soteriology, articulating *mokṣa* as a positive metaphysical event — the *dīkṣā*-mediated transformation of an enduring conscious soul into an eternally distinct *siddha* — rather than as the recognitional collapse of distinction.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Sangraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored short kārikā-treatise; not to be confused with Śāntarakṣita's later Buddhist Tattva-Saṅgraha.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Goodall et al. editions).

engaged_works.summary: A compact compendium of the Saiddhāntika *tattva*-hierarchy — the thirty-six *tattvas* from earth (*pṛthvī*) through *māyā* and *śuddha-vidyā* up to Śiva — read as the Saiddhāntika ontological architecture rather than as the non-dual emanation it later becomes in the Trika tradition (*Tattva-Saṅgraha* vv. 1–10, the foundational disposition; vv. 11–50 on the *tattva*-list). Sadyojyotis presents the *tattvas* as a real cosmological hierarchy: each is an objective level of being, not a phase of consciousness's self-display (*Tattva-Saṅgraha* vv. 51–80, against any *cit-mātra*-vāda reading). The text serves as the school's structural propaedeutic, showing how the three-fold *pati* / *paśu* / *pāśa* ontology articulates into the full *tattva*-cosmology, with *māyā-tattva* itself as the real material substrate from which the lower *tattvas* derive (*Tattva-Saṅgraha* on the *māyā-tattva* and the *aśuddha-mārga*). The contrast with the same *tattva*-list as it appears in Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* ([*TĀ* 8–11](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/8-11)) is precisely the contrast between dualist and non-dual readings of identical scriptural inheritance.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Trayanirnaya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored kārikā-treatise on the three principal tattvas (Śiva, soul, bonds).
engaged_works.summary: A focused treatise establishing the Saiddhāntika doctrine of *tattva-traya* — the three foundational realities Śiva (*pati*), soul (*paśu*), and bond (*pāśa*) — as eternally distinct, mutually irreducible, and jointly necessary for any coherent account of the world and the religious life (*Tattva-Trayanirṇaya* vv. 1–10, the foundational thesis). Sadyojyotis argues against monist alternatives: against the consciousness-only reading, which cannot account for the soul's *real* bondage (*TTN* vv. 11–25); against the world-as-illusion reading, which cannot account for *bhoga* or *karma* (*TTN* vv. 26–40); and against the no-Lord reading, which cannot account for the soul's purification (*TTN* vv. 41–55). The *tattva-traya* is therefore the minimum ontology a coherent religious metaphysics requires. The text is the school's most concentrated statement of its irreducibly pluralist commitment and is the natural starting point for understanding why the later non-dual Pratyabhijñā polemic, when it arrives with Somānanda ([*Śiva-Dṛṣṭi* 1.39–48](cite://somananda/shiva-drsti/1.39-48); *ŚD* 7.1–25), must engage Sadyojyotis on precisely this terrain.

engaged_works.title: Naresvara-Pariksa
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the major kārikā-treatise establishing the distinction of soul (nara) from Lord (īśvara), later commented on by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Watson 2006, The Self's Awareness of Itself; Goodall and Filliozat editions).

engaged_works.summary: The longest and most ambitious of Sadyojyotis's kārikā-treatises, in three *prakaraṇas*, devoted to establishing the eternal distinction between the soul (*nara*) and the supreme Lord (*īśvara*). The first *prakaraṇa* ([*Nareśvara-Parīkṣā* 1.1–60](cite://sadyojyotis/naresvara-pariksa/1.1-60)) establishes the existence of the eternally bound *paśu* as a real *cit*-substance, against Buddhist *anātman* (*NP* 1.10–25) and against materialist reductions; the second (*NP* 2.1–40) establishes the existence and necessary properties of *Īśvara* (Śiva) as omniscient, omnipotent, and transcendent of *māyā*, against atheist and Sāṃkhya alternatives; the third (*NP* 3.1–40) establishes the eternal distinction of soul from Śiva, against any identity-thesis. Crucial for the comparative work: Sadyojyotis here defends a positive theory of self-awareness (*sva-saṃvedana*) for the soul that is structurally distinct from both the Buddhist reflexivist account and from the later Pratyabhijñā doctrine of *vimarśa* as a divine power (*NP* 1.30–55, the constructive *sva-saṃvedana* argument: every cognition has its own self-luminous quality belonging to the *cit*-substance that has it, and this self-luminosity is *not* a borrowed mode of one universal awareness). Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II's *Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa* will, two centuries later, deploy this analysis polemically against the rising Pratyabhijñā position. The text is therefore the school's signature work and the natural focus of the dualist–non-dualist debate.

reception_notes: Sadyojyotis is the foundational dualist Saiddhāntika theologian; his works are the natural target of the later Pratyabhijñā polemic and the natural source for Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II's mature dualist defense. In the modern reception of Kashmir Śaivism his recovery (especially through Alex Watson, Dominic Goodall, and Pierre-Sylvain Filliozat) has corrected the popular impression that 'Kashmir Śaivism' is identical with the non-dual Trika; the dualist Saiddhāntika position is its older and equally serious sibling.
